FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (Year 3, 2019-2021), cross-sectional analysis of 9989 adolescents aged 11-14 years from 21 sites across the United States.

FT_IC2.evidence: Only cyberbullying victimization measured (dependent variable). Paper examines association between gender minority identity (independent variable) and cyberbullying (outcome). Cyberbullying alone is NOT a qualifying smartphone/SM exposure per B3. No qualifying smartphone or SM variable as exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analysis: OR 2.24 (95% CI 1.22-4.10, p=0.009) for transgender identity predicting cyberbullying victimization; OR 1.91 (95% CI 1.05-3.47, p=0.035) for gender-questioning.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports OR with 95% CI for transgender identification (yes: OR 2.24, 95% CI 1.22-4.10; maybe: OR 1.91, 95% CI 1.05-3.47) and p-values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data using logistic regression on cross-sectional Year 3 follow-up data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-text article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025, no indication of duplicate publication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not meet FT-EC7 criteria because: (a) SM/cyberbullying is NOT the dependent variable in analyses; gender identity is the predictor. (b) No longitudinal demographic→SM analysis structure. Does not meet binding criterion.
notes: This paper investigates cyberbullying victimization in transgender/gender-questioning adolescents from ABCD. Cyberbullying alone (without separate SM exposure measure) does NOT qualify per B3. Paper FAILS FT-IC2 because cyberbullying is the only measure and is not a qualifying smartphone/SM variable as an exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (Year 3, 2019-2021), descriptive analysis of 10,092 adolescents aged 11-15 years from 21 geographically diverse sites across the United States.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying SM variables: platform-specific SM use (TikTok 67.1%, YouTube 64.7%, Instagram 66.0%, Snapchat 60.3%), SM time on weekdays/weekends, secret SM accounts (6.3%), and total number of SM accounts (mean 3.63). Per B1 and platform-specific use criterion.

FT_IC3.evidence: Purely descriptive statistics. Paper reports prevalence percentages and Chi-square/t-tests for demographic stratification (age, sex) but NO inferential test linking SM variables to external outcome. No regression coefficient, correlation, or statistical test of SM predicting a non-SM outcome. Per B10—SM is only characterized descriptively, not tested against external outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: No regression coefficients, OR, RR, HR, d, r, F, t, χ², η², R², p-values, or CI reported for SM↔outcome associations. Only descriptive percentages and prevalence data in tables.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data using descriptive methods (Chi-square, t-tests) on cross-sectional Year 3 follow-up data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-text article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025, no indication of duplicate publication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not analyze SM as dependent variable predicted by demographics. Instead, paper describes SM prevalence stratified by demographics (age, sex). No longitudinal prospective analysis. Does not meet FT-EC7 structure.
notes: Paper FAILS FT-IC3 and FT-IC4. This is a purely descriptive epidemiological study documenting SM platform prevalence and patterns in early adolescents. No inferential statistical test linking SM to any non-SM outcome. SM variables are not tested against external outcomes; they are only characterized by demographics. Violates both FT-IC3 (no inferential test of SM↔non-SM) and FT-IC4 (no extractable numeric coefficients).

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, prospective analysis of Year 2 and Year 3 follow-up (2018-2021, baseline 2016-2018), 8006 adolescents mostly 11-12 years old at Year 2, recruited from 21 sites across the United States.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen modalities: (1) social media (0.7 ± 1.6 h/d) per B1; (2) texting (0.7 ± 1.5 h/d) per B1 (smartphone texting qualifies); (3) video chatting (0.5 ± 1.3 h/d) per B1 (video chat on phone qualifies). All three are qualifying smartphone/SM variables. Non-qualifying modalities (TV/movies, videos, single/multi-player games, internet) are also included but do not disqualify per B1 (separate coefficients reported for each modality).

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses testing prospective associations between screen time (Year 2) and substance use experimentation (Year 3). For social media: AOR 1.20 (95% CI 1.14-1.26) for any substance; AOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.10-1.21) for alcohol; AOR 1.23 (95% CI 1.14-1.32) for nicotine; AOR 1.22 (95% CI 1.13-1.33) for cannabis. For texting: AOR 1.18 (95% CI 1.12-1.24) for any substance; AOR 1.14 (95% CI 1.08-1.20) for alcohol; AOR 1.16 (95% CI 1.06-1.26) for nicotine; AOR 1.18 (95% CI 1.08-1.29) for cannabis. For video chatting: AOR 1.09 (95% CI 1.03-1.16) for any substance; AOR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.22) for nicotine; AOR 1.13 (95% CI 1.03-1.24) for cannabis.

FT_IC4.evidence: Figure 1 and results text report AOR (adjusted odds ratios) with 95% CI for all screen modalities vs. substance use outcomes. All numeric extractable: AOR, 95% CI, p-values indicated by asterisks (*) for Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted significance.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis of ABCD Study data using logistic regression models predicting substance use experimentation in Year 3 from screen use in Year 2, adjusting for covariates and baseline substance use.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-text article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2025, no indication of duplicate publication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not meet FT-EC7 criteria. SM/screen modalities are INDEPENDENT variables (Year 2), not dependent variables. Analysis structure is screen use→substance use outcome, not demographics→SM. Prospective but does not fit FT-EC7 binding structure (demographics-only→SM as DV, longitudinal).
notes: Paper MEETS all FT-IC1 through FT-IC6. Prospective study of screen use (including social media, texting, video chatting—all qualifying per B1) predicting substance use experimentation. Multiple qualifying smartphone/SM variables with separate coefficients reported. Strong prospective design. Does not apply FT-EC7 (SM is IV, not DV; outcome is substance use, not SM).
